summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between grocery store self-checkout machines and open source software development, arguing that both succeed when they align self-interest with participation. He uses the self-checkout metaphor to explain why people willingly do unpaid work: they care most about their own outcomes. Atwood then critiques OpenOffice.org's declining developer community, attributing it to bureaucratic barriers that undermine the self-service nature of open source. He argues that open source projects fail when they create obstacles to contribution, destroying the intrinsic motivators of self-determination and autonomy. The post concludes that struggling open source projects should blame themselves, not the community, for failing to lower barriers to participation.
key_points: Self-checkout works because nobody cares about getting you checked out as much as you do - it's perfect alignment of self-interest
Open source software development is the ultimate self-service industry, where users can directly fix their own problems
OpenOffice.org's declining contributor base stems from 'demotivating and dysfunctional process steps' that prevent code from being included
Destroying self-determination and autonomy on an open source project makes it no better than closed source software
If programmers - who are inclined to understand and love this stuff - won't contribute, the project has fundamentally failed
money_quotes: Nobody else in that store cares about getting Jeff Atwood checked out nearly as much as Jeff Atwood does.
We programmers fundamentally grok the hoops that the self-service checkout machines make customers jump through.
Open source software development is the ultimate self-service industry.
You've created a self-service checkout machine so painful to use, so awkward to operate, that it gives the self-service concept a bad name.
Once you destroy the twin intrinsic motivators of self-determination and autonomy on an open source project, you're no better off than you were with traditional closed source software.
If you're having difficulty getting software developers to participate in your open source project, the community isn't failing your project. Your project is failing the community.
key_insight: Open source projects thrive or die based on how well they enable self-service contribution - when bureaucracy makes participation painful, the project fails its community, not the other way around.
